fragments.text: In the Epic of Gilgamesh, the snake steals the plant of youth and immediately sheds its skin, sliding away renewed, leaving the husk behind.
fragments.source: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI (Mesopotamia, c. 2100 BCE)

fragments.text: Ovid's Marsyas, flayed by Apollo, cries 'Why do you tear me from myself?' — his whole body one wound, the skin peeled away entire.
fragments.source: Ovid, Metamorphoses VI (Rome, 1st c. CE)

fragments.text: In Ovid's Metamorphoses, thin bark closes over Daphne's breast as she runs; skin hardens to rind, hair to leaves, the girl still warm underneath.
fragments.source: Ovid, Metamorphoses I (Rome, 1st c. CE)

fragments.text: In Orkney selkie tales, the seal-wife finds her hidden sealskin in the locked chest, slips it on, and returns to the sea forever.
fragments.source: Orkney/Shetland selkie folklore (recorded 19th c.)

fragments.text: In the Noh play Hagoromo, a fisherman withholds the celestial maiden's feather robe; without it she cannot ascend — the garment is the border between worlds.
fragments.source: Hagoromo, Noh theater (Japan, 15th c.)

fragments.text: In Barrie's Peter Pan, Peter's shadow, torn off by a slamming window, is rolled up in a drawer until Wendy sews it back to his heels.
fragments.source: J.M. Barrie, Peter Pan (London, 1904/1911)

fragments.text: In Heda's Dutch vanitas still lifes, a lemon's peel hangs off the table edge in one unbroken spiral, pale flesh exposed, rind curling into shadow.
fragments.source: Willem Claesz Heda, banketje still lifes (Netherlands, 1630s)

fragments.text: Gijsbrechts's 1670 trompe-l'oeil shows only the back of a painting — stretcher, canvas, inventory tag; turning it over would reveal just another back.
fragments.source: Cornelis Norbertus Gijsbrechts, Reverse of a Framed Painting (Copenhagen, 1670)

fragments.text: Seventeenth-century quodlibet trompe-l'oeil engravings render printed sheets whose corners curl up with false shadows, forever promising a liftable page and another image beneath.
fragments.source: Quodlibet / letter-rack trompe-l'oeil prints (Flanders and Netherlands, 17th c.)

fragments.text: Magritte's The Human Condition: a canvas on an easel exactly continues the landscape behind it — removing the painting would remove the view itself.
fragments.source: René Magritte, La Condition humaine (Belgium, 1933)

fragments.text: In Perrault's Donkeyskin, the princess flees wrapped in a donkey's hide, sun-and-moon dresses hidden beneath; a ring baked into a cake unmasks her.
fragments.source: Charles Perrault, Peau d'Âne (France, 1694)

fragments.text: In Holbein's The Ambassadors, a grey smear across the floor resolves into a skull only from one oblique standpoint; step aside and death vanishes.
fragments.source: Hans Holbein the Younger, The Ambassadors (London, 1533)

fragments.text: A matryoshka splits at the waist to release a smaller identical self, again and again, until the last doll — solid wood, unopenable.
fragments.source: Russian matryoshka, Zvyozdochkin and Malyutin (Sergiev Posad, 1890s)

fragments.text: The German advent calendar rations revelation: twenty-four paper doors, each hiding one small picture, each forbidden until its numbered day arrives.
fragments.source: German Adventskalender, Gerhard Lang's printed versions (Munich, c. 1908)

fragments.text: Victorian tunnel books stage the 1843 Thames Tunnel as stacked cut-paper layers seen through a peephole — a whole tunnel compressed into a paper accordion.
fragments.source: Thames Tunnel peepshow books (London, 1840s)

fragments.text: Apian's Astronomicum Caesareum (1540) builds the heavens from stacked rotating paper discs — volvelles — layers of the cosmos turned by a silk thread.
fragments.source: Petrus Apianus, Astronomicum Caesareum (Ingolstadt, 1540)

fragments.text: Pollock's toy theaters — 'penny plain, twopence coloured' — shrink Drury Lane to a tabletop proscenium; cardboard actors slide on wires into a miniature storm.
fragments.source: Pollock's juvenile drama sheets (London, 19th c.)

fragments.text: In the Tale of Genji, the lady slips free of her robe to escape Genji, who keeps the shed garment and names her Utsusemi — cicada shell.
fragments.source: Murasaki Shikibu, Genji Monogatari, ch. 3 'Utsusemi' (Japan, c. 1008)

fragments.text: The arm reliquary of Charlemagne shapes its jeweled case as the arm it contains — the body's outside remade in silver around the bone inside.
fragments.source: Arm reliquary of Charlemagne, Aachen Cathedral Treasury (1481)

fragments.text: A Noh ko-omote mask, tilted down, grieves; tilted up, it smiles — one carved wooden face holding both expressions, released by angle alone.
fragments.source: Noh ko-omote mask tradition (Japan, from 14th c.)

fragments.text: Vesalius-era fugitive sheets: anatomical engravings with paper flaps — lift the chest door of the printed man and his viscera open layer by layer.
fragments.source: Anatomical fugitive sheets, Heinrich Vogtherr and successors (Strasbourg, 1538 onward)

fragments.text: Houdon's écorché (1767): a flayed man stands calmly, arm outstretched, every muscle exposed — the skin gone, the inside now the permanent outside.
fragments.source: Jean-Antoine Houdon, L'Écorché (Rome/Paris, 1767)

fragments.text: The Archimedes Palimpsest: tenth-century geometry scraped from parchment and overwritten as a prayer book — the older text ghosting beneath, recoverable by raking light.
fragments.source: Archimedes Palimpsest (Byzantium, 10th c.; overwritten 13th c.)

fragments.text: Cornell's Medici Slot Machine: a Renaissance boy stands behind crosshair-etched glass among compartments of marbles and jacks — a princeling sealed in a penny arcade.
fragments.source: Joseph Cornell, Medici Slot Machine (New York, 1942)

fragments.text: In Carroll's Alice in Wonderland, behind a low curtain waits a fifteen-inch door; through it Alice sees the loveliest garden — at her size, unreachable.
fragments.source: Lewis Carroll, Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, ch. 1 (Oxford, 1865)

fragments.text: In Carroll's Through the Looking-Glass, the mirror over the mantel softens to bright silvery mist, and Alice climbs through the melting glass into the reversed room.
fragments.source: Lewis Carroll, Through the Looking-Glass, ch. 1 (Oxford, 1871)

fragments.text: Borges's Aleph: a small iridescent sphere on a cellar stair, two or three centimetres wide, containing every point of the universe seen at once.
fragments.source: Jorge Luis Borges, El Aleph (Buenos Aires, 1945)

fragments.text: Calvino's Olinda, in Invisible Cities, grows in concentric rings like a trunk; at the center a new tiny Olinda swells, pushing the old outward.
fragments.source: Italo Calvino, Le città invisibili (Italy, 1972)

fragments.text: In Gogol's The Nose, the barber finds Kovalyov's nose baked inside his breakfast loaf; days later it rides a carriage in a state councillor's uniform.
fragments.source: Nikolai Gogol, Nos (St. Petersburg, 1836)

fragments.text: In Grimm's Allerleirauh, the princess folds three dresses — gold as sun, silver as moon, glittering as stars — into a nutshell, then cloaks herself in a thousand furs.
fragments.source: Brothers Grimm, Allerleirauh, KHM 65 (Germany, 1812)
